source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.4 File Tree, pwd and cd (pp. 40-44)

questions.q: In the Linux file tree, what is the root of the single hierarchical tree?
questions.why: Linux uses one inverted tree whose root is the directory '/'; there are no separate drive letters.

questions.q: What is the difference between an absolute and a relative path?
options: An absolute path starts from the root /; a relative path starts from the current directory
An absolute path starts from the current directory; a relative path starts from /
An absolute path may only point at directories; a relative path only at files
There is no difference; they are two names for the same thing
questions.why: Absolute paths traverse from / (current directory irrelevant); relative paths traverse from wherever you currently are.

questions.q: Every directory, even an empty one, contains at least two references. What are '.' and '..'?
options: '.' is the directory itself; '..' is its parent directory
'.' is the parent; '..' is the directory itself
'.' is the root; '..' is the home directory
'.' is a hidden file; '..' is a symbolic link
questions.why: '.' refers to the directory itself and '..' to its parent; a relative path can begin with ./ or ../.

questions.q: The current directory is /home/groupA/bob. What relative path reaches /home/groupA/alice/myfile?
questions.why: From bob you go up one level with '..' into groupA, then down into alice to myfile: ../alice/myfile.

questions.q: Print the absolute path of the current directory.
questions.why: pwd (Print Working Directory) shows the absolute path of where you currently are.
cd (Change Directory) moves you through the tree; here to the absolute path /tmp.

questions.q: Move up one level, into the parent of the current directory.
questions.why: '..' is the parent directory, so 'cd ..' moves one level up the tree.

questions.q: Return to your own home directory, typing as little as possible.
questions.why: cd with no argument returns you to your login (home) directory.
